summary: A 12th-century English civil war (also called the Nineteen-Year War or Nineteen-Year Winter) fought between Stephen of Blois and his cousin Empress Matilda over succession to the English throne, sparked by the 1120 sinking of the White Ship, which drowned Henry I's only legitimate son, William Adelin. Clues most often identify it through the Treaty of Wallingford that ended it, the Peterborough Chronicle's lament that "Christ and his saints slept," the Rout of Winchester and First Battle of Lincoln, and Empress Matilda's legendary escape across the frozen River Thames from a siege at Oxford Castle. The war takes its name from the lawless, chaotic breakdown of royal governmental authority that characterized it, and it ended with a settlement that paved the way for the accession of Henry II and the Plantagenet dynasty.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: A 12th-century English civil war and succession struggle -- also called the Nineteen-Year War or Nineteen-Year Winter -- fought between Stephen of Blois and Empress Matilda across England and Normandy, taking its name from the lawless nature and lack of governmental presence that characterized the period. It ultimately led to the formation of the House of Plantagenet through the eventual accession of Henry II.

name this civil war between the forces of Stephen of Blois and Empress Matilda.

clues.clue: Named for the lawless nature and lack of governmental presence that characterized the period

examples: name this conflict in mid-1100s England and Normandy, in which the forces of Matilda of Flanders and Stephen of Blois duked it out, leading to the formation of the House of Plantagenet.

works.name: Causes: The White Ship Disaster
works.description: The war was sparked by the November 1120 sinking of the White Ship on a return voyage from Normandy to England, which drowned William Adelin, King Henry I's only legitimate son and heir, leaving Henry without a male successor and touching off a succession crisis between his nephew Stephen of Blois and his daughter Empress Matilda.

clues.clue: Began/was sparked by the sinking of the White Ship (November 1120), which drowned William Adelin, Henry I's only legitimate son and heir
examples: This conflict occurred after William Adelin died when the White Ship sank in the English Channel, leaving Henry I without a legitimate son.
It was initiated by the sinking of a ship killing William Adelin, the only heir of Henry I.

works.name: The Siege of Oxford Castle
works.description: One of the war's most memorable episodes: besieged at Oxford Castle during a snowstorm, Empress Matilda escaped by dressing in white and walking across the frozen River Thames (locally called the Isis) to camouflage herself against the snow, making her way to the castle of her ally Brian fitz Count. During the same siege, one side placed siege engines atop Jew's Mount to bombard the castle.

clues.clue: Besieged at Oxford Castle, Empress Matilda escaped during a snowstorm by dressing in white and crossing the frozen River Thames (the Isis) to camouflage herself against the snow
examples: The leader of one side escaped from Oxford Castle by wearing white and crossing a frozen moat during a snowstorm.
During this conflict, a white-clad woman hid against the snow as she crossed the frozen Thames to flee Oxford Castle.

clues.clue: She fled to the castle of her ally Brian fitz Count
examples: One leader during this war travelled to Brian fitz Count's castle after escaping a siege by walking across a frozen river in a white robe.

clues.clue: One side placed siege engines atop Jew's Mount during the siege
examples: One side during this war placed siege engines atop Jew's Mount in one battle.

works.name: The Rout of Winchester
works.description: At the Rout of Winchester, Empress Matilda's commander Robert of Gloucester was captured while covering her retreat; he was later exchanged for King Stephen, who had himself been captured and imprisoned after the earlier Battle of Lincoln.

clues.clue: Robert of Gloucester was captured at the Rout of Winchester and later exchanged for the captive King Stephen
examples: Robert of Gloucester, a commander in this war, was captured after the Rout of Winchester.
Robert of Gloucester was captured in this war's Rout of Winchester, and he was exchanged for a king who had been imprisoned since losing the Battle of Lincoln.

works.name: The Battle of the Standard
works.description: David I of Scotland invaded northern England in support of his niece Empress Matilda, but the elderly, 70-year-old Archbishop Thurstan of York led English forces to an early victory at the Battle of the Standard. The battle took its name from a mobile, flagpole-bearing cart -- similar to the Italian carroccio -- that carried a consecrated host and saints' banners and marked the center of William of Aumale's battle line; Aelred of Rievaulx's account of the battle attributes a pre-battle speech to the baron Walter Espec.

clues.clue: David I of Scotland invaded northern England in support of Matilda, but the elderly (70-year-old) Archbishop Thurstan led English forces to victory
examples: During this war, the 70-year-old bishop Thurstan thwarted a Scottish invasion at the Battle of the Standard.
Thurstan led one side's forces at this conflict's Battle of the Standard.

clues.clue: Took its name from a mobile, flagpole-bearing cart carrying a consecrated host and saints' banners (similar to the Italian carroccio) that marked the center of William of Aumale's line
examples: One battle in this conflict took its name from a mobile flagpole-bearing cart that marked the center of the line of William of Aumale.

examples: another battle in this conflict is the subject of a text by Aelred of Rievaulx that attributes a pre-battle speech to Walter Espec.

works.name: The First Battle of Lincoln
works.description: At the First Battle of Lincoln (1141), King Stephen's besieging forces were surprised by a relieving army; he was surrounded by Angevin knights and taken prisoner by one William Kahmenes, briefly allowing Empress Matilda to rule as queen before Stephen was exchanged for Robert of Gloucester after the Rout of Winchester.

clues.clue: Stephen was surrounded by Angevin knights and taken prisoner by William Kahmenes, briefly allowing Empress Matilda to rule
examples: In one battle during this conflict, a ruler was surrounded by Angevin knights before being taken prisoner by William Kahmenes.

clues.clue: Stephen's capture and imprisonment after this battle led to his later exchange for Robert of Gloucester, captured at the Rout of Winchester
examples: he was exchanged for a king who had been imprisoned since losing the Battle of Lincoln.

clues.clue: The king's capture at this battle eventually led to the Treaty of Wallingford and the accession of the future "Curtmantle," Henry II
examples: culminated with the king's capture at Lincoln and the subsequent treaty of Wallingford, which eventually led to the accession of a royal nicknamed "Curtmantle."

works.name: Local Uprisings and Minor Figures
works.description: The war saw constant local betrayals and shifting allegiances. Geoffrey de Mandeville switched sides after his property, including Walden Castle, was seized and he was arrested, engaging in repeated changes of side and outlaw activity that may be referenced in the Peterborough Chronicle's eyewitness account. Baron Walter Espec initially supported one force but changed his mind after his nephew Jordan de Bussey was abandoned at the fortress of Wark, while Baldwin de Redvers led a separate rebellion during which many unauthorized "adulterine" castles were built, as chronicled by Robert of Torigni; William le Gros began construction of Scarborough Castle during the war, which was forfeited once the war ended. After the fighting had officially concluded, one disgruntled commander plundered church lands near Bury St. Edmunds and choked to death on his meal.

clues.clue: Geoffrey de Mandeville switched sides after his property, including Walden Castle, was seized and he was arrested; his frequent side-switching and outlaw activity may be referenced in the Peterborough Chronicle's eyewitness account
examples: That man, Geoffrey de Mandeville, may be referenced in the eyewitness account of this conflict in the Peterborough Chronicle.
The frequent changing of sides and outlaw activity by Geoffrey of Mandeville during this conflict led to its most famous description in the Peterborough Chronicle.

works.name: Contemporary Chronicles
works.description: Several contemporary chronicles serve as key sources for the war. The Peterborough Chronicle's final continuation offers the war's most famous description, lamenting that "men said openly that Christ and his saints slept: we suffered for nineteen winters for our sins." The Historia Novella was the main historical work focused on the conflict, though it breaks off unfinished due to its author's death, while William of Newburgh's Historia rerum Anglicarum is a prime source for the 18-year civil war that resulted after the White Ship disaster let Stephen of Blois seize the throne.

clues.clue: The Peterborough Chronicle's final continuation laments that "men said openly that Christ and his saints slept: we suffered for nineteen winters for our sins"
examples: During this conflict, "men said openly that Christ and his saints slept: we suffered for 19 winters for our sins" according to the "Second Continuation" of the Peterborough Chronicle.
The Peterborough Chronicle states that, during this conflict, "men said openly that Christ and his saints slept."

clues.clue: The Historia Novella was the main historical work focused on the war, though it breaks off unfinished due to its author's death
examples: This conflict was the main focus of the Historia Novella, which breaks off due to its author's death.

examples: William of Newburgh's work is a prime source for this 18-year period of civil war in England, which resulted after the White Ship Disaster allowed Stephen of Blois to seize the throne.

works.name: Aftermath: The Treaty of Wallingford
works.description: The war ended with the Treaty of Wallingford (1153), which allowed Stephen to remain king for the rest of his life but recognized Empress Matilda's son, Henry of Anjou, as his heir over Stephen's own son Eustace, whose earlier attempted installation as king had been rejected by the Catholic Church. During the war, one side had also lost the towns of Oxford and Stamford even after the peace agreement was signed.

clues.clue: Ended with the Treaty of Wallingford, which recognized Empress Matilda's son, Henry of Anjou, as heir to the English throne over Stephen's own son
examples: The Treaty of Wallingford also provided for the eventual accession of Henry II of the Plantagenet line.
The Treaty of Wallingford brought an end to this notoriously chaotic time in English history, defined by civil warring between Stephen of Blois and Empress Matilda's supporters.

clues.clue: One man involved attempted to install his son Eustace as king, but this was rejected by the Catholic Church
examples: One man involved in this conflict attempted to install his son Eustace as king but was rejected by the Catholic Church.

clues.clue: One side lost the towns of Oxford and Stamford even after the peace agreement was signed at Wallingford
examples: During this conflict, one side lost Oxford and Stamford even after a peace agreement was signed at Wallingford.

comprehensive_summary: The Anarchy was a 12th-century English civil war (also called the Nineteen-Year War or Nineteen-Year Winter) fought between Stephen of Blois and his cousin Empress Matilda over the English succession, taking its name from the lawless breakdown of royal governmental authority that characterized it. It was sparked by the November 1120 sinking of the White Ship, which drowned William Adelin, King Henry I's only legitimate son, leaving no male heir. At the Siege of Oxford Castle, Empress Matilda famously escaped a siege during a snowstorm by dressing in white and crossing the frozen River Thames (the Isis) to the castle of her ally Brian fitz Count, while another key episode, the Rout of Winchester, saw her commander Robert of Gloucester captured and later exchanged for King Stephen, who had himself been captured at the First Battle of Lincoln (1141) after being surrounded by Angevin knights and taken prisoner by William Kahmenes -- briefly allowing Matilda to rule. Elsewhere, David I of Scotland invaded in support of his niece Matilda but was defeated by the elderly Archbishop Thurstan at the Battle of the Standard, named for a mobile flagpole cart bearing a consecrated host, at which Aelred of Rievaulx's account attributes a pre-battle speech to Walter Espec. The war also saw constant local betrayals -- Geoffrey de Mandeville's repeated side-switching after the seizure of Walden Castle, Walter Espec's own change of allegiance after his nephew Jordan de Bussey was abandoned at Wark, Baldwin de Redvers's rebellion amid a wave of unauthorized "adulterine" castle-building chronicled by Robert of Torigni, and William le Gros's construction of Scarborough Castle. Contemporary chronicles, including the Peterborough Chronicle (whose famous line laments that "Christ and his saints slept"), the unfinished Historia Novella, and William of Newburgh's Historia rerum Anglicarum, are key sources for the period. The war ended with the 1153 Treaty of Wallingford, which let Stephen remain king for life but recognized Matilda's son, Henry of Anjou, as his heir over Stephen's own son Eustace (whose installation as king had been rejected by the Church), paving the way for the Plantagenet dynasty.
recursive_suggestions: Empress Matilda -- appeared as her own dense answerline (daughter of Henry I, brother's death in the White Ship, marriage to Geoffrey of Anjou) independent of the war itself; excluded here per the different-actual-answer rule and worth a standalone figure page.
Stephen of Blois -- appeared as his own dense answerline (nephew of Henry I, opposition to Matilda, son Eustace of Boulogne) independent of the war; excluded here and worth a standalone figure page.

The White Ship (disaster) -- appeared as its own dense answerline (captain Thomas Fitzstephen, race against the king's ship, William Adelin's death) independent of the war itself; excluded here and worth a standalone topic.

First Battle of Lincoln -- appeared as its own answerline with independent clue content (Robert of Gloucester's relieving army); most of its content was still usable here via Anarchy-answer clues, but it could support a standalone page given further depth.

William of Newburgh -- recurring chronicler with independent biographical clue content (the "green children of Woolpit," his Historia rerum Anglicarum) surfaced in this search; excluded as a different actual answer.

Ambiguity flag: "the Anarchy at Samarra," a 9th-century Abbasid period of instability, surfaced as a same-named but wholly unrelated answerline (Samarra) and was excluded entirely -- filter to English/medieval History when re-querying.
